steps.title: Welcome to Day 7
steps.body: You have reached the last day of your first week. Today is about creativity. You will learn how to use AI to generate ideas, explore options, and break through the blank-page problem — fast.

steps.title: AI is an ideas machine
steps.body: AI has absorbed millions of books, articles, and conversations. When you ask it to brainstorm, it can generate ten ideas in ten seconds — without judgment, without fatigue, and without running out of options.

steps.title: You know this feeling
steps.body: You need to name a product. Or pitch a fresh angle for a project. Or figure out how to solve an old problem in a new way. You sit. You stare. Nothing comes. AI does not have that problem.

steps.title: What to brainstorm with AI
steps.body: Name something (product, project, event). Find angles on a topic. Generate options for a decision. Explore "what if" scenarios. Create solutions to a problem you are stuck on. AI works for all of these.

steps.title: A strong brainstorm prompt
steps.prompt: Act as a creative director. I am launching a small online business that teaches people to make homemade sourdough bread. Generate ten name ideas that feel warm, approachable, and memorable. For each name, write one sentence explaining why it works. Then give me your top three picks.

steps.title: What made that prompt effective
steps.body: It gave AI a role, described the context clearly, asked for a quantity (ten ideas), requested a brief rationale for each, and asked for a recommendation (top three). Constraints and quantities push AI toward better output.

steps.title: Quick check
steps.question: Which brainstorm prompt will get the most useful set of ideas?
options: I am planning a surprise birthday party for my mum who loves gardening and tea. Give me eight theme ideas that combine both interests. Keep them realistic for a home setting.
What are some birthday party ideas?
feedback: Too vague — AI has nothing to anchor the ideas to.
Excellent. Specific context (who, what they love), a quantity (eight), and a constraint (realistic, home setting) produce targeted, useful ideas.
General ideas will come back — but they will not reflect anything specific about the person.
This gives AI nothing to work with. Constraints and context always improve creativity.

steps.title: Another check
steps.question: You are stuck on how to market a new service. What is the best next step?
options: Keep thinking on your own until an idea comes.
Ask AI: 'Give me some ideas for marketing.'
Ask AI to act as a marketing strategist, describe your service in detail, and ask for ten creative angles aimed at your target audience.
feedback: That works eventually, but AI can give you ten starting points in seconds.
Possible, but without context about your service or audience, the ideas will be generic.
Exactly. Role, context, quantity, and audience focus produce ideas you can actually use.
A friend is great — but so is combining that with AI's ability to generate lots of options quickly.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: When brainstorming with AI, you should only use its best idea and ignore the rest.
feedback: Actually, many great ideas come from combining or remixing several AI suggestions. Review all the options before deciding.
Right. The full list is the point. You might combine two ideas, or an unexpected option might be exactly right.

steps.title: Explore options
steps.question: Pick a real decision you are weighing right now — big or small. Ask AI to generate options two ways: first freely with no constraints, then with your specific constraints included. Compare. Which list is more useful?

steps.title: The "ten ideas" trick
steps.body: Whenever you are stuck, try: "Give me ten ideas for [thing]." Then ask: "Now give me ten more unusual ones." The second list is often where the interesting answers live. People (and AI) start safe and get more creative with repetition.

steps.title: Build a brainstorm prompt

fields.label: What do you want to brainstorm?
fields.placeholder: e.g. names for my new podcast about personal finance

fields.label: Who is this for, or what vibe do you want?
fields.placeholder: e.g. young adults who find money confusing but want to do better

fields.label: How many ideas do you want?

steps.template: Act as a creative director. I need to brainstorm {{topic}}. Audience/vibe: {{audience}}. Generate {{quantity}} ideas. For each idea, write one sentence explaining why it fits. Then tell me your top three.

steps.title: Copy your brainstorm prompt
steps.body: Copy your prompt and paste it into ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Grok to see what it generates.

steps.title: Paste your brainstorm results
steps.body: Paste the ideas AI gave you here so we can save them to your work.

questions.label: Which lesson this week felt most useful to you?
What is one AI habit you plan to keep using after this week?

questions.question: What improves the quality of a brainstorm prompt most?
options: Making the request as short as possible
Adding specific context, a quantity, and your audience or vibe

questions.question: Asking for ten ideas is better than asking for one because it gives you options to combine and remix.
To push AI toward more unusual ideas, after the first list ask it to give you ______ more ideas.

steps.title: Week 1 complete
steps.body: You did it. In seven days you learned to ask clear questions, assign roles, write better, research faster, plan smarter, get sharper feedback, and brainstorm on demand. These are skills most people never develop. Well done — and see you in Week 2.
